answer:
**Iqbal's "Maa ka Khwab" (Mother's Dream; *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29) and the Old English "The Wife's Lament" (*Old English Elegies*, ch. 3) both foreground a woman's anguished inner world amid separation from a beloved male figure—son in the former, husband/lord in the latter.**[^1][^2]

The affective register of "The Wife's Lament" is relentlessly desolate and raw, steeped in **exilic grief, grim endurance, and hopeless longing**. The speaker dwells on her isolation in a "wooded grove, / under an oak tree in this earth-cave," weeping "summer-long day" for "paths of exile" and "many sorrows," her "heart’s-care" unrelieved.[^3] She evokes a world of "deepest misery," "bitter boroughs," and "endless sorrow," where even "young man [must] be grim of spirit," masking pain behind a "cheerful face."[^4] Betrayal by kinsmen and forced parting amplify a stoic yet crushing despair: "Far and near I must / suffer the feud of my dearest love."[^5] No resolution pierces this bleak monotone of woe.

Iqbal's poem, by contrast, layers **maternal anxiety and turmoil with dream-induced dread, reproachful sorrow, and a revelatory turn**. The mother's "deep turmoil" erupts in a night vision of fearful darkness, trembling steps, and her son trailing a silent procession of lamp-bearing boys—his own lamp absent.[^6] Her cry pierces: "Your absence leaves me in deep turmoil; / I string each day a garland of my tears," accusing filial neglect.[^7] Yet the son's rebuke shifts the register: her "weep[ing] and cry[ing]" extinguishes his lamp, implying her excessive grief hinders his path.[^8] This injects moral clarity, tempering raw emotion with corrective insight.

**Both poems pulse with separation's sting—longing for the "beloved" (son/husband)—but diverge sharply: the Wife's affect is a static wail of irrevocable loss, evoking Anglo-Saxon fatalism; the Mother's is dynamic, fearfully possessive yet chastened by dream-wisdom, aligning with Iqbal's motivational ethos.** The Wife broods in perpetual exile; the Mother awakens to self-restraint. Common threads of tearful isolation yield to cultural contrasts: unrelenting elegy versus edifying vision.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/).
[^2]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [¶5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-c1cc32).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [¶6](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-956fe7).
[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3, [¶4](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-66ceaf).
[^6]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29, [¶¶1–7](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-761301).
[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29, [¶12](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-c4bed6).
[^8]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29, [¶¶14–15](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-4feffc).
